section.title: The Prayer Against the Accuser
section.sectionRationale: One of the fiercest imprecatory psalms, this prayer of a slandered man entrusts vengeance entirely to God rather than taking it into his own hands, and its language concerning the wicked man's office is later applied by the apostles to Judas Iscariot.
section.thesis: When love is repaid with hatred and lies, the godly response is not private revenge but honest lament that hands the accuser over to God's justice, resting all hope of rescue on the LORD's steadfast love alone.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the cry against slander, verses 1-5; the imprecation against the accuser, verses 6-20; the psalmist's own frailty and plea, verses 21-25; the prayer for public vindication, verses 26-29; and the closing vow of praise, verses 30-31.

verses.text: For the choirmaster. A Psalm of David. O God of my praise, be not silent.
For wicked and deceitful mouths open against me; they speak against me with lying tongues.
They surround me with hateful words and attack me without cause.
In return for my love they accuse me, but I am a man of prayer.
They repay me evil for good, and hatred for my love.

units.label: The prayer against the accuser
units.note: imprecation entrusted to God, applied to Judas' office in Acts 1:20

verses.text: Set over him a wicked man; let an {{satan|accuser}} stand at his right hand.
When he is tried, let him be found guilty, and may his prayer be regarded as sin.
May his days be few; may another take his position.
May his children be fatherless and his wife a widow.
May his children wander as beggars, seeking sustenance far from their ruined homes.
May the creditor seize all he owns, and strangers plunder the fruits of his labor.
May there be no one to extend kindness to him, and no one to favor his fatherless children.
May his descendants be cut off; may their name be blotted out from the next generation.
May the iniquity of his fathers be remembered before the LORD, and the sin of his mother never be blotted out.
May their sins always remain before the LORD, that He may cut off their memory from the earth.
For he never thought to show kindness, but pursued the poor and needy and brokenhearted, even to their death.
The cursing that he loved, may it fall on him; the blessing in which he refused to delight, may it be far from him.
The cursing that he wore like a coat, may it soak into his body like water, and into his bones like oil.
May it be like a robe wrapped about him, like a belt tied forever around him.
May this be the LORD's reward to my accusers, to those who speak evil against me.
But You, O GOD, the Lord, deal kindly with me for the sake of Your name; deliver me by the goodness of Your {{chesed|loving devotion}}.
For I am poor and needy; my heart is wounded within me.
I am fading away like a lengthening shadow; I am shaken off like a locust.
My knees are weak from fasting, and my body grows lean and gaunt.
I am an object of scorn to my accusers; when they see me, they shake their heads.

units.label: A plea for public vindication
units.note: let them know this is Your hand

verses.text: Help me, O LORD my God; save me according to Your loving devotion.
Let them know that this is Your hand, that You, O LORD, have done it.
Though they curse, You will bless. When they rise up, they will be put to shame, but Your servant will rejoice.
May my accusers be clothed with disgrace; may they wear their shame like a robe.

units.label: A vow of praise
units.note: He stands at the right hand of the needy

verses.text: With my mouth I will thank the LORD profusely; I will praise Him in the presence of many.
For He stands at the right hand of the needy one, to save him from the condemners of his soul.

satan.gloss: An adversary or accuser, often in a legal setting standing at a defendant's side to bring charges. David asks that the accuser's own tactic, false accusation, be turned back on him in a just courtroom reversal.

chesed.gloss: Steadfast, covenant love and loyalty. David's entire hope for rescue, both from slander and from harm, rests not on his own righteousness but on God's chesed alone, verses 21 and 26.

basic.context: David is surrounded by liars who repay his love and prayers with hatred and false accusation. Rather than taking revenge himself, he hands his accuser over to God in the fiercest imprecatory language in the Psalter, then turns to confess his own frailty and beg for rescue.
basic.hermeneutics: The curses are not personal vindictiveness but a legal appeal, asking the righteous Judge to let the accuser's own schemes fall back on his own head, a pattern of justice repeatedly affirmed elsewhere in Scripture. Peter later reads verse 8 as prophetic of Judas' office being filled by another, Acts 1:20.
basic.originalLanguages: David asks that a satan, an accuser, stand at his enemy's right hand in the same way accusers stood in Israel's courts, verse 6, a reversal of what has been done to him. His only appeal for rescue in verses 21 and 26 is God's chesed, His steadfast covenant love, not any merit of his own.
basic.typology: David, the anointed but falsely accused king, foreshadows Christ, who was falsely accused before Caiaphas and Pilate yet entrusted Himself to the Father who judges justly; the wicked accuser of this psalm anticipates Judas, whose betrayal of the greater David is explicitly read through this psalm in Acts 1:20.
basic.christ: Jesus, like David, was hated in return for love and falsely accused by wicked and deceitful mouths, yet He did not revile in return but entrusted Himself to God who judges justly, 1 Peter 2:23. Peter applies this psalm's language of the accuser's office being filled by another directly to Judas Iscariot, Acts 1:20, showing that the psalm's justice reaches even to the betrayer of the true Son of David.
basic.god: God is the righteous Judge who alone has the right to repay evil, so David surrenders his case entirely into God's hand rather than seizing vengeance himself. God is also gracious and near to the poor and needy, standing at the right hand of the very needy one this psalm portrays, ready to save him from his condemners.
basic.crossReferences: Acts 1:20 quotes verse 8 directly of Judas' replacement in the apostolic office. Romans 12:19 commands believers to leave vengeance to God rather than repay evil for evil. 1 Peter 2:23 shows Christ's own pattern of entrusting Himself to the Father rather than retaliating under false accusation.
basic.oneStory: A falsely accused servant of God hands his case entirely to the righteous Judge rather than seizing vengeance himself; this pattern reaches its full weight in Christ, who was hated for His love, and whose betrayer, Judas, is judged by the very words of this psalm, while the true King is finally vindicated and saved by the God who stands at the right hand of the needy.

context.literary: A tightly structured lament: invocation and complaint, verses 1-5; an extended imprecation against the accuser, verses 6-20; personal lament and plea, verses 21-25; petition for vindication, verses 26-29; and a closing vow of praise, verses 30-31, with the center section, verses 16-20, naming the accuser's specific crimes as the hinge of the whole poem.
context.historical: The tone fits any of David's seasons of betrayal by a trusted associate, whether under Saul's court, Doeg's treachery, or Absalom's rebellion, and reflects an ancient Near Eastern legal setting where an accuser, satan, formally stood at a defendant's side in court.
context.canonical: Among the Psalter's imprecatory psalms alongside 35, 69, and 137, this psalm's curses are quoted directly in the New Testament, Acts 1:20, giving it unusual canonical weight as prophetic Scripture concerning the betrayer of the Messiah.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To model how a falsely accused believer brings the full weight of injustice to God in prayer, asking the righteous Judge to reverse the accuser's schemes onto his own head, rather than executing personal vengeance.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes David's specific plea against a specific betrayer; it prescribes the abiding pattern of entrusting justice to God rather than repaying evil for evil, while the concentrated curses of verses 6-19 are best read as legal petition for God's justice, not a template for personal cursing of one's own enemies today.

debates.question: How should Christians read the severe curses of verses 9-15, which extend even to the wicked man's children and family line?

views.label: The curses reflect the corporate solidarity and covenant justice categories of the ancient Near East, entrusted entirely to God to execute or withhold
views.case: David never acts on these words himself; he hands the entire case to God's righteous judgment, and Scripture elsewhere holds that children are not put to death for their fathers' sins, Deuteronomy 24:16, showing the prayer trusts God's just discernment rather than demanding automatic execution.

views.label: The severity is quoted by the apostles as literally fulfilled in Judas, showing the words carry genuine prophetic and judicial force rather than being merely rhetorical
views.case: Acts 1:20 applies verse 8 directly and specifically to Judas' fate and the filling of his office, indicating the psalm's curses were understood by the earliest church as real, Spirit-inspired words with concrete historical fulfillment.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that vengeance belongs to God alone, never to the individual believer, and that this psalm's harshest language is prayer entrusted to God's throne, not a script for personal retaliation, Romans 12:19.

study.originalLanguages: David asks that a satan, an accuser, stand at his enemy's right hand, verse 6, turning the very tactic used against him back upon the wicked man in a legal reversal. His only ground for rescue, repeated in verses 21 and 26, is God's chesed, His steadfast covenant love, never his own merit or vengeance.

typology.type: The falsely accused servant who does not retaliate, verses 1-5
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ, who was reviled yet did not revile in return, but entrusted Himself to Him who judges justly, 1 Peter 2:23.

typology.type: The accuser whose office is given to another, verse 8
typology.fulfillment: Applied directly by Peter to Judas Iscariot, whose apostolic office was filled by Matthias, Acts 1:20, 26.

study.christ: Jesus is the ultimate fulfillment of the falsely accused righteous sufferer of this psalm, hated in return for love, yet, unlike the imprecations David voices, Christ prayed for His executioners' forgiveness, Luke 23:34, entrusting Himself entirely to the Father who judges justly. Strikingly, the New Testament reads this very psalm as prophetic of Judas Iscariot, whose betrayal of the true Son of David and subsequent judgment fulfill the psalm's words about the accuser's office passing to another, Acts 1:20.
study.god: God is the righteous Judge to whom all vengeance rightly belongs, so much so that David surrenders his entire case into God's hand rather than acting himself. He is also near and gracious to the poor and needy, standing at the right hand of the afflicted to save him from those who condemn his soul.

crossReferences.note: Peter applies verse 8 directly to Judas Iscariot's fate and the filling of his apostolic office by another.
Commands believers to leave vengeance to God rather than repay evil, the very pattern David models by handing his case to the LORD.
Christ, reviled, did not revile in return but entrusted Himself to the Father who judges justly, the fullest answer to this psalm's plea.

study.oneStory: A man repaid evil for good hands his cause entirely to the righteous Judge rather than seizing revenge himself, and this pattern culminates in Christ, who was hated for His love and did not retaliate, while the very words of this psalm are shown by the apostles to have reached forward to judge Judas, the betrayer of the true and greater David, whom God finally stands to save at His right hand.

quiz.q: How does David describe the treatment he has received from his accusers in verses 4-5?

options.text: They repay his love with accusation and hatred, evil for good.
options.note: This stark reversal, love met with hatred, is the wound that drives the entire psalm.

options.text: They ignore him completely and say nothing against him.
options.note: The opposite is true; wicked and deceitful mouths actively speak against him.

options.text: They repay his kindness with equal kindness.
options.note: David explicitly says evil for good, hatred for love, not reciprocated kindness.

options.text: They only oppose him in private, never publicly.
options.note: The text does not limit the accusation to private settings; it describes open, surrounding hostility.

quiz.q: What does David do instead of taking revenge himself against his accuser?

options.text: He hands the case entirely to God in prayer, asking the righteous Judge to act.
options.note: The entire imprecation, verses 6-20, is addressed to God, not carried out by David's own hand.

options.text: He organizes his own supporters to retaliate.
options.note: David takes no such action; the psalm is entirely a prayer, not a plan of retaliation.

options.text: He quietly accepts the slander without any response.
options.note: David responds vigorously in prayer, though not through the accepted silence this option suggests.

options.text: He appeals to the king's court for a legal ruling.
options.note: His appeal is to God directly, described in legal imagery, but the court is heavenly, not an earthly tribunal.

quiz.q: What is David's only stated ground for rescue in verses 21 and 26?

options.text: God's loving devotion, His chesed, not David's own merit.
options.note: Both verses appeal explicitly to God's steadfast love as the sole basis for deliverance.

options.text: David's flawless righteousness and good record.
options.note: David appeals to God's character, not his own merit, as the ground for rescue.

options.text: The number of allies David has gathered.
options.note: The psalm shows David isolated and scorned, not surrounded by supportive allies.

options.text: His wealth and social status.
options.note: David describes himself as poor and needy, the opposite of relying on wealth or status.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament apply verse 8 of this psalm?

options.text: Peter applies it to Judas Iscariot and the filling of his apostolic office by Matthias.
options.note: Acts 1:20 quotes this verse directly regarding Judas' betrayal and replacement.

options.text: It is never referenced anywhere in the New Testament.
options.note: Acts 1:20 quotes this very verse directly.

options.text: Paul applies it generally to all false teachers in Galatia.
options.note: The specific New Testament application is to Judas in Acts 1, not a general reference in Galatians.

options.text: It is applied to the Roman soldiers who crucified Jesus.
options.note: The specific apostolic application is to Judas' betrayal and office, not the soldiers at the cross.

quiz.q: How does Christ ultimately go beyond this psalm's pattern of entrusting judgment to God?

options.text: Though reviled, He did not revile in return, and even prayed for His executioners' forgiveness.
options.note: 1 Peter 2:23 and Luke 23:34 show Christ both entrusting Himself to the Father and actively praying mercy for His accusers.

options.text: He personally cursed every one of His accusers by name.
options.note: Christ's response was forgiveness and entrustment to the Father, not personal cursing.

options.text: He avoided the false accusations by fleeing Jerusalem.
options.note: Christ did not flee; He submitted to false accusation and crucifixion.

options.text: He demanded that Pilate immediately punish His accusers.
options.note: Christ made no such demand; He entrusted Himself to the Father who judges justly.

questions: David is repaid evil for the good and love he showed others. Where have you experienced this kind of unjust reversal, and how did you respond?
Instead of taking revenge, David hands his entire case to God in prayer. What would it look like to fully surrender a desire for payback to God this week rather than acting on it yourself?
David's only appeal for rescue is God's loving devotion, not his own merit. Are you tempted to believe God owes you help because of your own goodness, rather than resting on His grace alone?

This psalm's harshest words are ultimately read by the apostles as pointing to Judas, the betrayer of the true King. How does seeing God's justice reach even to Christ's betrayer strengthen your trust that no injustice done to you is finally hidden from Him?

Christ, unlike the imprecation of this psalm, prayed forgiveness for His accusers even while suffering. Where is God calling you to move from merely trusting Him with justice toward also actively forgiving those who have wronged you?

translationNotes.note: Accuser renders the Hebrew satan, a legal adversary who stood at a defendant's side in ancient courts; David asks that this role be turned back on the one who has falsely accused him.
May another take his position is quoted in Acts 1:20 concerning the office vacated by Judas Iscariot and filled by Matthias.

The Hebrew text reads seeking sustenance, while the ancient Greek Septuagint reads may they be driven, a minor textual variant that does not change the verse's picture of the wicked man's family reduced to homeless want.

No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson) was confirmed for this specific psalm this session, so sermon status is none.
